title: Turkey Diplomatic Realignment Act
plain_summary: This bill tells the State Department to change how it handles Turkey. Right now, Turkey is managed by the group that deals with European countries. The bill says Turkey should be moved to the group that handles Middle Eastern countries instead. This change must happen within 90 days because Congress thinks Turkey acts more like a Middle Eastern country now. In five years, the State Department has to tell Congress how well this change worked.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Turkey Diplomatic Realignment Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Findings
sections.text: Congress has found the following facts. Turkey sits in a special location between Europe and the Middle East. This location affects American security, economic, and diplomatic interests in both areas. Right now, the State Department handles Turkey through its Bureau of European and Eurasian Affairs. But Turkey is heavily involved in Middle Eastern affairs and security issues. Moving Turkey to the Bureau of Near Eastern Affairs would better match diplomatic strategy and help with regional cooperation. In recent years, Turkey has moved away from the European Union and NATO (a military alliance with the United States and other countries). This is shown by Turkey changing its partnerships and making political changes at home that go against European democratic values. European institutions have increasingly pushed Turkey aside from full membership. They have delayed talks about Turkey joining and raised concerns about human rights, democratic government, and rule of law. Turkey is building stronger partnerships with non-western countries like Russia and China. Turkey is also trying to join BRICS (a group of emerging economies). This shows Turkey is shifting its priorities away from working with western countries. The Turkish government is increasingly working with Islamist groups. Turkey plays a role in conflicts in places like Syria and Libya. Turkey has strained relations with Israel. These things have created deeper concerns about whether Turkey can be trusted as a long-term western partner. Moving Turkey to a different bureau sends a diplomatic message. It shows that Turkey has fewer chances of joining Europe. It encourages national discussion within Turkey about its future role in the world, especially since Turkey feels left out of European decision-making. Turkey has provided political and logistical support to Hamas (a Palestinian militant group). Turkey hosts Hamas members and helps them raise money. This has further damaged Turkey's relationships with western allies and added to concerns about which side Turkey is on in the region. The United States still wants a strong relationship with Turkey. But America must adjust how it deals with Turkey diplomatically to recognize changing realities. This ensures American regional policy stays consistent with changing security and economic situations.

sections.title: Section 3 Administrative Reassignment
sections.text: Within ninety days after this law is passed, the Secretary of State must move responsibility for Turkey from the Bureau of European and Eurasian Affairs to the Bureau of Near Eastern Affairs within the State Department. Within five years after this law is passed, the Secretary must send a report to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs and the Senate Committee on Foreign Relations. The report must describe how effective this change was and what the consequences were. The report must also provide recommendations for any other changes regarding who is responsible for Turkey. Nothing in this section stops the Secretary from changing or stopping the use of regional bureaus if needed to advance American diplomatic goals.
